coredata.dc:title: Variation in the responses of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized from native coconut husk and coconut husk-derived charcoal
coredata.dc:creator: Takahama, Satoru
Kitamura, Masaki
Ide, Yuki
Umemura, Kazuo
coredata.dc:description: We compared the autoclave method for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from plain coconut husks and husk-derived charcoal in the current study. Although several publications reported the synthesis of CQDs from the two types of husks, no prior reports were available for direct comparison of the two CQDs. The filtrated CQDs were characterized using Excitation-Emission Matrix (EEM) spectroscopy. In comparison to plain husk, the charcoal material was effective for obtaining high concentrations of CQDs because larger amounts of the charcoal husk could be packed into a vessel for autoclave treatment. EEM provided a thorough evaluation of the correlations between the excitation and emission spectra. CQDs synthesized from charcoal and plain exhibited markedly different EEM profiles. Between the two CQDs, we observed an opposite response of fluorescent (FL) intensity and peak wavelengths to pH variation in the aqueous solvent. In contrast, there was no difference in terms of temperature dependence. As an illustration of biological applications, the quenching of papain enzyme self-FL was also investigated. When papain molecules were combined with CQDs derived from plain husk, significant quenching of the papain self-FL was observed, but this phenomenon was not observed when CQDs derived from charcoal husk were used. Our research clarified the differences of CQDs from two types of coconut husks. The knowledge is helpful to apply the CQDs for various biological applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot (CQD)
Coconut-husk
Excitation-emission matrix (EEM)
Atomic force microscopy (AFM)
Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR)
Papain enzyme

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financially supported by JST SICORP Japan (grant number JPMJSC19E1 ).
Kazuo Umemura reports financial support was provided by Japan Science and Technology Agency .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Concentration dependence of FL spectra of fabricated CQDs from charcoal and plain coconut-husks. (a) Charcoal. (b) Plain. (c) Intensities at each peak versus concentrations.

EEM maps with different pH. (a)–(c) Charcoal. (d)–(f) Plain. (a) and (d) pH 3.0. (b) and (e) pH 7.0. (c) and (f) pH 9.0. Range of color bars were 0.00–1.00 and 0.00–0,24 for charcoal and plain, respectively.

Cross-section analysis of the EEM maps. (a) Excitation spectra of charcoal CQDs. (b) Emission spectra of charcoal CQDs. (c) Excitation spectra of plain CQDs. (d) Emission spectra of plain CQDs. (e) Intensities at each peak. (f) Wavelength shift of each peak at different pH values. (g) Numerical analysis of peak wavelengths.

Characterization of CQDs by UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy ((a) and (b), Raman spectroscopy ((c) and (d)), and FTIR spectra ((e) and (f)). (a), (c), and (e) CQDs from charcoal husk. (b), (d), and (f) CQDs from plain husk.
AFM images of CQDs on a mica surface. (a) Charcoal, 2 μm × 2 μm scan areas. (b) Charcoal, 500 nm × 500 nm scan areas. (c) Plain, 2 μm × 2 μm scan areas. (d) Plain, 500 nm × 500 nm scan areas.
Temperature dependence of FL spectra of CQDs from charcoal (a) and plain (b) coconut-husks.
Quenching of self-FL from papain enzyme. (a) With charcoal. (b) With plain. (c) Quenching ratios.

head.ce:title: Variation in the responses of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized from native coconut husk and coconut husk-derived charcoal

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biophysics Section, Department of Physics, Faculty of Science Division II, Tokyo University of Science, 1-3 Kagurazaka, Shinjuku, Tokyo, 162-8601, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Biophysics Section, Department of Physics, Faculty of Science Division II, Tokyo University of Science, 1-3 Kagurazaka, Shinjuku, Tokyo, 162-8601, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We compared the autoclave method for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from plain coconut husks and husk-derived charcoal in the current study. Although several publications reported the synthesis of CQDs from the two types of husks, no prior reports were available for direct comparison of the two CQDs. The filtrated CQDs were characterized using Excitation-Emission Matrix (EEM) spectroscopy. In comparison to plain husk, the charcoal material was effective for obtaining high concentrations of CQDs because larger amounts of the charcoal husk could be packed into a vessel for autoclave treatment. EEM provided a thorough evaluation of the correlations between the excitation and emission spectra. CQDs synthesized from charcoal and plain exhibited markedly different EEM profiles. Between the two CQDs, we observed an opposite response of fluorescent (FL) intensity and peak wavelengths to pH variation in the aqueous solvent. In contrast, there was no difference in terms of temperature dependence. As an illustration of biological applications, the quenching of papain enzyme self-FL was also investigated. When papain molecules were combined with CQDs derived from plain husk, significant quenching of the papain self-FL was observed, but this phenomenon was not observed when CQDs derived from charcoal husk were used. Our research clarified the differences of CQDs from two types of coconut husks. The knowledge is helpful to apply the CQDs for various biological applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) made from green materials such as non-edible plant parts are an intriguing research topic [1–5]. The use of such residues is beneficial for the sustainable growth of the world. Another advantage of CQDs is their high biocompatibility, which is a result of their synthesis from natural products [6–8]. Due to the insoluble nature of metal or semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) in aqueous solutions, their surfaces should be coated with hydrophilic molecules for biological applications [9–11]. In the case of CQDs fabricated using green materials, the hydrophilic carboxyl groups persist even when method hydrothermal route of synthesis is used [12–14].

Numerous plants have been investigated as carbon sources in the form of green materials [3,4,15,16]. For instance, starch from corn and other plants was a significant feedstock [17–19]. Rice, coconut, and other seed husks have also been widely used [20–24]. Numerous authors also made use of charcoals made from various plants [25–30].

Autoclaving is the most frequently used method of synthesis [31–33]. Carbon sources are sealed in a decomposition vessel with pure water or other aqueous solutions and annealed for several hours at temperatures between 150 and 200 °C. This straightforward hydrothermal procedure is sufficient to generate CQDs. As one of the alternative methods, laser ablation is an effective method for producing CQDs [34–36]. It was recently reported that microwave plasma treatment of the sources is also sufficient for the synthesis of CQDs [37–39]. Purification of prepared CQDs was accomplished using centrifugation, filtration, and/or dialysis [40,41].

For biological applications, such as antibacterial device fabrication, protein sensor evaluation, and antidepressant drug evaluation, vitamin D detection was used [42–46]. Numerous surface functionalization methods for CQDs have been proposed to enable biological applications [47–49].

We used the autoclave method to fabricate CQDs from both plain and charcoal coconut husks in this work. The use of coconut husks is advantageous from the viewpoints sustainable development goals (SDGs) because coconut-husks are one of the typical wastes after taking edible parts. The autoclave method can be processed only with a vessel and a heater. It is one of the advantages of the method to be widely prevailed the CQD techniques. Although numerous authors reported CQDs from plain coconut husk, only one author reported on charcoal coconut husk [44,50–53]. The synthesis of CQDs from charcoal coconut husk was accomplished via laser ablation rather than autoclave. There has been no direct comparison of CQDs derived from charcoal and plain coconut husks synthesized using the autoclave method under the same conditions. Numerous reports indicate that CQDs have been synthesized using coconut fiber, [54] coconut water, [55–57] coconut milk, [58] coconut oil, [59], and coconut petiole residues. [60] Additionally, we evaluated the produced CQDs using Excitation-Emission Matrix (EEM) spectroscopy, despite the fact that absorbance and fluorescent (FL) spectra were previously measured independently [61, 62]. Moreover, the simultaneous acquisition of absorbance, FL, and excitation spectra was achieved using the EEM. Additionally, comparing the two CQDS extracted from plain and charcoal coconut husks was deemed efficient. Based on the direct comparison of the two types of CQDs with plain and charcoal coconut husks, we found that the CQDs from plain material had higher ability to quench fluorescence from papain molecules although charcoal material was advantageous to produce huge amounts of CQDs. The quenching phenomenon might be applied to detect enzyme reactions with small amount of proteins and/or substrates. If enzymes reaction can be detected without dye-labelled substrates, it will be a convenient method.

Charcoal made from coconut husks was purchased (Yashicoal-L, Taihei Chemical Industrial Co., Ltd., Osaka, Japan). The plain coconut husk was obtained from (Ora Farm, Gunma, Japan). The papain enzyme was obtained from (164–00172, Fujifilm Wako Chemicals, Osaka, Japan). The remaining chemicals were of an analytical grade.

To prepare CQDs, 25 g of charcoal coconut husk or 1 g of plain coconut husk were combined with 50 mL of pure water in a sealed decomposition vessel (HU-100, SAN-AI Kagaku Co. Ltd., Aichi, Japan). The vessel was annealed at 200 C for 3 h for charcoal and 6 h for plain, then centrifuged for 20 min at 15000 rpm (21500×g). Following centrifugation, 70% of the supernatant was stored as raw CQD suspensions. To facilitate purification, raw suspensions were filtered through a 0.45 μm Millipore filter (Millipore Ireland BV, Cork, Ireland).

The excitation-emission matrix (EEM) of the prepared CQDs was determined using a Duetta spectrometer (HORIBA, Ltd., Kyoto, Japan). All samples were subjected to the following parameters. For charcoal samples, the excitation and emission wavelengths were 250–400 nm and 350–600 nm, respectively. Additionally, the excitation and emission wavelengths for both charcoal and husk samples were 250–500 nm and 350–600 nm, respectively, for the purpose of preparing EEM maps for direct comparison. The band path and exposure time were set to 5 nm and 0.40 s, respectively. Absorbance and FL of synthesized CQDs were also measured by the same spectrometer for basic evaluation.

To determine concentration dependence, samples were diluted with pure water to 5, 15, 25, 35, 45% of the filtered stock suspensions. The CQD suspensions were diluted twice with pure water and sealed in a cuvette to examine temperature dependence. Temperatures of the suspension were varied in the order listed below. 25 → 35→45 → 55→65 → 55→45 → 35→25 → 15→25 °C. To determine the pH of the CQD suspensions, appropriate amounts of hydrochloric acid (HCl) or sodium hydroxide were added to adjust the pH to 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 9.0, and 11.0. (NaOH). Correspondingly, the stock CQD suspension was diluted twice with pure water and HCL/NaOH.

FL was used to determine papain adsorption on CQD surfaces (HORIBA, Ltd., Kyoto, Japan). The samples were excited at 280 nm, and the FL spectra were recorded at wavelengths ranging from 300 to 500 nm. The band path and exposure time were set to 5 nm and 0.50 s, respectively. To begin, 1 mL of papain solution (1 mg/mL papain, 20 mM phosphate buffer solution, pH 6.8) was measured. Subsequently, the CQD suspension was gradually added to the same cuvette (10 μL at a time) until the total volume reached 50 μL. To assess reproducibility, all EMM measurements were repeated three times with independent samples.

The IRT-5000 was used to perform Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (JASCO Co. Tokyo, Japan). 50 μL of CQD suspension was deposited and dried on a cover-slip. To obtain sufficient absorbance values, each sample was deposited 15 times. The FTIR spectra were measured between 700 and 4000 cm−1 (4.0 cm−1 resolution, 128 times accumulation).
Raman spectra was obtained by RAMANtouch i VIS-NIR (Nanophoton Corporation, Osaka, Japan). 100 μL of CQD suspensions were deposited on coverslips, and then, measured after drying.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was carried out using MFP-3D microscope (Asylum Research, Santa Barbara, CA) with AC mode in air. For charcoal and plain CQDs, the stock suspensions of CQDs were diluted 10000 and 20 times with pure water, respectively. Subsequently, 10 μL of CQD suspension was deposited and dried on a freshly cleaved mica and used for analysis.

CQDs were made using charcoal and plain coconut husks. The two materials were simply sealed in a decomposition vessel containing 50 mL of pure water and then annealed at 200 °C for 3 or 6 h before being filtered through a 0.45 μm Millipore filter. Although several papers described the synthesis of CQDs from charcoal and plain coconut husks, each publication used one of the two materials. There is no previous report that compares the two materials in terms of CQD preparation. Based on previous publications and our preliminary experiments, 25 g of husks were annealed for 3 h in the charcoal samples. Additionally, 1 g of husks was annealed for 6 h in the plain samples. One of the reasons of the difference is due to the bulk density of the two materials. Although 25 g of charcoal husks could be put in the vessel, it was impossible in the plain husks due to the low density.

The linearity of the FL intensities of CQDs synthesized with 25 g charcoal and 1 g plain is shown in Fig. 1 . We measured the synthesized CQDs at various dilution ratios up to 20 times dilution. Based on the initial amounts of coconut-husks, concentration range of the CQDs from charcoal was 0.225 to 0.025 g/mL. That of the CQDs from plain husks was 0.009 to 0.001 g/mL. Excitation wavelengths were 315 and 365 nm for charcoal and plain based CQDs, respectively. Maximum FL intensities were obtained with the excitation wavelengths. In Fig. 1, peak intensity values near 430 nm were plotted (c). FL intensity changed linearly with dilution with pure water in the case of the charcoal sample. In the plain sample, the FL intensity was exceeded at higher concentrations. To obtain the maximum FL intensities, cross-sections were created from EEM maps. As a result, the Ex wavelengths used to obtain the cross-sections varied slightly between samples. This will be discussed in greater detail in Fig. 3. FL intensity of filtrated CQD from charcoal husk without dilution (0.5 g/mL) were around 230 a.u. That of CQD from plain husk was around 360 a.u. without dilution (0.02 g/mL). The values for 1 g/mL samples might be estimated to 720 and 11500 for charcoal and plain based CQDs, respectively, although FL intensities were not exactly proportional in the plain CQDs (inserts in Fig. 1(c)). As a rough estimate, FL yields of CQDs from plain husk was higher than that from charcoal husk.

EEM maps revealed a wealth of information about the CQD spectral region. On the maps, the FL locations had shoulders surrounding the FL peaks. The shoulders were asymmetrical in the plain CQDs. The shoulder of the charcoal CQDs was widened to accommodate longer Em wavelengths [see arrow in Fig. 2(b)]. Even with very short excitation wavelengths, CQDs emit at a variety of wavelengths (around 315 nm). Em wavelengths had a nearly linear relationship with Ex wavelengths in the pure samples. The peculiar phenomenon may imply that CQDs made of ordinary material are optically responsible. Obtaining this data is one of the benefits of EEM measurements.

Fig. 3 depicts cross-sections of the EEM maps depicted in Fig. 2. Each cross-section had a different ex wavelength. For example, a cross-section of charcoal at pH 3.0 was obtained at Ex 325 nm because the Ex wavelength 325 nm achieved the maximum FL intensity of the CQDs at 453 nm (see the table in Fig. 3). It is clear that strong FL intensities were obtained in CQDs made from charcoal material at pH 7.0, 9.0, and 11.0. At pH 3.0 and 5.0, those were less than half. In CQDs made from plain material, the opposite results were obtained. At pH 9.0 and 11.0, peak FL intensities were significantly reduced. On the other hand, there was no evidence of peak shift as a function of pH [Fig. 3(f)]. Although the mechanism underlying this opposite response is unknown, it is useful information for selecting appropriate CQDs for the purposes of the research. For the mechanism of pH dependence of CQDs emissions, Hao et al. suggested that defect-bound trions in CQDs are one of the key factors for the pH dependence although it is not sufficient information to explain the difference between the charcoal CQDs and the plain CQDs [50]. Here we point out two specific features of our data for future researches. One of the specific features of our data is non-proportional FL changes. The data suggest that there are threshold pH levels between pH 5 and pH 7 both for the charcoal and plain samples. Another specific point is that the drastic changes of FL intensities of the CQDs from plain husks. As shown in Fig. 3(d) and (g), FL intensity at pH 3.0 (1533 ± 60) was 8.3 times larger than that at pH 11.0 (183 ± 6) in the case of the plain based CQDs. In the charcoal based CQDs, FL intensity at pH 9.0 (6432 ± 37) was 2.7 times larger than that at pH 3.0 (2427 ± 83). It suggests the CQDs from plain husks were more sensitive on pH conditions.

The fabricated CQDs were characterized by UV–vis spectra, FTIR spectra, and Raman spectra. Fig. 4 (a) and (b) show absorbance spectra of fabricated CQDs (100 times dilution). In the case of plain based CQDs, clear absorbance peak at 278 nm was obtained (Fig. 4(b)). Contrarily, complicated peaks were observed in the charcoal based CQDs (Fig. 4(a)). It seems that CQDs from plain husk had better structures. Fig. 4(c) and (d) show Raman spectra of the charcoal and plain based CQDs, respectively. Two peaks around 1404 and 1565 cm−1 corresponding to D band (sp3) and G band (sp2) of CQDs, respectively [51]. The peaks were clear in the plain based CQDs (Fig. 4(d)), but it was obscure in the charcoal based CQDs (Fig. 4(c)). The results corresponded to the absorbance spectroscopy. In the case of FTIR (Fig. 4(e) and (f)), peaks around 3198 and 1098 cm-1 could be assigned to the adsorption bands of the OH- stretching vibration mode [51]. The peaks about 1595 and 1405/1349 cm-1 could be attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration (COO-), respectively. Those peaks were observed both of charcoal and plain based CQDs.

AFM was used to examine the filtered CQD samples (Fig. 5 ). Distribution of CQDs were observed in large area images both in plain and charcoal samples (Fig. 5(a) and (c)). Spherical molecules with a height of 0.5–2 nm were clearly visible in both charcoal and plain samples by magnified scanning (Fig. 5(b) and (d)). The FTIR spectrum revealed several typical CQD peaks previously reported in the literature [52]. The broad peaks at 3198 cm−1 in charcoal and 3123 cm−1 in can be attributed to carboxyl group stretching and vibration. Peaks at 1595 and 1603 cm−1 can be attributed to the asymmetric stretching and vibration of COO−-. The fingerprint region of CQDs isolated from plain coconut husk was almost identical to that reported previously [52]. It was quite different for CQDs derived from charcoal coconut husk than it was for plains. Although no previous work has been published on CQDs synthesized from charcoal coconut husk via the autoclave method, the difference in FL profiles between the two types of CQDs may be due to structural differences revealed by FTIR spectroscopy.

We investigated the temperature dependence of the FL peak intensities of two CQDs (Fig. 6 ). As a result, at higher temperatures (65 °C), the FL intensities were quenched, and the response was reversible. There was no discernible difference in performance between charcoal and plain. The error bars on plain samples were larger. This could be originated from lower concentrations of the plain CQDs. The range of temperatures were selected for potential applications such as detection of enzyme reactions. Some specific enzymes such as papain from torrid zones work even at 65 °C.

The quenching of papain enzymes was demonstrated in order to assess the potential for biosensing applications. The enzyme, papain, is a major proteinase that is widely used in the treatment of meats, the preparation of beers, and a variety of other applications [63, 64]. It is well established that papain reveals that FL originated from its amino acids, particularly tyrosine. [65] Xiao et al., demonstrated that when papain molecules were bound to semiconductor QDs, the FL was quenched [53]. They suggested that dynamic quenching (collisional process) and/or static quenching (ground state complex formation) were involved in the quenching mechanism.

We examined the quenching of papain enzymes using both CQDs from charcoal and plain husks in this work. When a solution of 1 mg/mL papain was excited at 280 nm, the FL intensity was nearly 5000 a.u. Then, CQDs were gradually added to the papain solution (10 μL at a time) using charcoal or plain coconut husks. No significant quenching of papain FL was observed in the case of charcoal CQDs [Fig. 7 (a)]. However, when plain CQDs were used, papain FL was quenched in proportion to the increase in CQDs [Fig. 7(b)]. Concentration of papain was always 1 mg/mL, and amounts of CQDs were varied. The data suggest that CQDs synthesized from plain coconut husks may have applications in biosensing. The relationship between papain concentration and FL intensities is depicted in Fig. 7(c). In the case of CQDs derived from plain coconut husk, the quenching ratio did not increase linearly with the papain concentration. The sensitive responses of the plain based CQDs corresponded to that observed in pH dependence (Fig. 3). In addition, the results indicated that CQDs could be used in place of CdTe QDs. The CQDs formed from plain material were sufficient to quench the self-FL of papain molecules. As to the stability of CQDs, we observed the reversible responses for the varied temperatures. It also suggests the CQDs were stable in terms of time. One of the potential applications is detection of enzyme reactions using the quenching ability.

CQDs were synthesized in comparison to charcoal and plain coconut husks. Although both CQDs were prepared from coconut husks, their optical properties were quite different. EEM was an efficient method for determining the optical properties of CQDs in detail. CQDs derived from plain husk were effective in quenching self-FL of papain enzymes. Our research provided helpful information for the selective use of CQDs derived from green materials.
This study was financially supported by JST SICORP Japan (grant number JPMJSC19E1).
Satoru Takahama: Investigation, Methodology, Visualization. Masaki Kitamura: Investigation, Data curation. Yuki Ide: Investigation. Kazuo Umemura: Writing – original draft, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:
Kazuo Umemura reports financial support was provided by Japan Science and Technology Agency.
